analyst_observations: The only assertion the baseline consistently fails is 'categorizes-by-severity' — all 3 baseline runs used ad-hoc Medium/Low/Info schemes instead of the structured Critical/Stale/Missing categories the skill teaches.

Both configurations perform equally well on the other 6 assertions, suggesting the baseline model is already capable of thorough doc review — the skill's main value-add is in structuring the output consistently.

The with-skill version caught the return type mismatch (datetime vs NextContactSchedulerResponse) in eval 1 at Critical severity, while the baseline rated the same issue as Medium. This severity calibration difference could matter for triage.

The baseline for eval 0 actually found an additional valid issue (scriptManagerTypes.ts naming ambiguity) that the with-skill version marked as 'no issue'. The skill may be slightly more conservative about flagging edge cases.
Token usage is comparable (~3% more with skill), so the skill doesn't cause significant cost overhead.

The with-skill version for eval 2 caught a merge conflict risk (S1) that the baseline also caught — both identified this independently, suggesting it's a strong signal in the code rather than a skill-specific insight.

Non-discriminating assertions: 6 of 7 assertions pass regardless of skill presence. The skill's quantitative advantage is narrow (1 assertion), suggesting the value is more about consistency and calibration than fundamentally different analysis.
